era_context: Late 12th century. The Crusader states are declining; Saladin is consolidating Ayyubid power. In Europe, feudalism is at its height with powerful regional lords. In Asia, the Jin Dynasty controls northern China; the Khmer Empire is flourishing; Japan is entering the Genpei War. Islamic civilization is experiencing a cultural and scientific flourishing.

events.title: Beginning of the Genpei War in Japan
events.description: In 1180, the Genpei War (1180–1185) began, a civil conflict between the Minamoto and Taira clans for control of Japan. The war was triggered by the death of Emperor Takakura and disputes over succession and power between the two military clans. Minamoto no Yoritomo raised his banner in the Kanto region in eastern Japan, initiating a conflict that would fundamentally transform Japanese politics. The Genpei War represented a shift from the dominance of the imperial court and Fujiwara regency to the rise of military government (bakufu). The conflict saw the development of samurai military culture and the codification of warrior ethics. The war lasted five years and culminated in the establishment of the Kamakura shogunate in 1185.
key_figures: Minamoto no Yoritomo (military leader, future shogun)
Taira no Kiyomori (Taira clan leader, died 1181)

sources.name: Genpei Seisui Ki (Record of the Genpei War)

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Genpei War in 1180 is well-documented in Japanese sources. The date is secure.
cross_references: 1185-001 (End of Genpei War and establishment of Kamakura shogunate)

events.title: Death of Taira no Kiyomori
events.description: In 1180 (or early 1181, depending on the source), Taira no Kiyomori, the powerful leader of the Taira clan, died. Kiyomori had dominated Japanese politics for decades, establishing the Taira clan as the dominant military force in Japan. His death marked a turning point in the Genpei War, as the Taira clan lost its most capable leader at a critical moment. The succession of leadership to Taira no Munemori, who was less capable than Kiyomori, contributed to the eventual Taira defeat. Kiyomori's death is sometimes dated to 1181, but 1180 is also cited in some sources. His death symbolized the decline of the Taira clan and the rise of the Minamoto.
events.certainty_note: Taira no Kiyomori's death is traditionally dated to 1181, though some sources place it in 1180. The exact year is uncertain by ±1 year.

events.title: Saladin's Consolidation of Ayyubid Power
events.description: In 1180, Saladin (Ṣalāḥ ad-Dīn Yūsuf ibn Ayyūb) was consolidating his control over the Ayyubid confederation, unifying Egypt, Syria, and parts of Mesopotamia under his authority. By 1180, Saladin had largely eliminated rival Ayyubid princes and was establishing a centralized military and administrative structure. This consolidation was essential to his later campaigns against the Crusader states. Saladin's unification of the Islamic world in the Levant and Egypt created the military and economic resources necessary for the major offensives against the Crusaders that would culminate in 1187. Saladin's consolidation also represented a shift from fragmented regional powers to a unified Islamic state capable of coordinated military action.

sources.name: Imad ad-Din al-Isfahani, Conquest of Jerusalem
Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad, Life of Saladin

events.certainty_note: Saladin's consolidation of Ayyubid power is well-documented in contemporary Islamic sources. The process spanned several years, with 1180 marking a significant phase in this consolidation.

events.title: Frederick Barbarossa's Rule of Holy Roman Empire
events.description: In 1180, Frederick I Barbarossa was in the 26th year of his reign as Holy Roman Emperor (1152–1190). The year 1180 falls within a period of relative stability in the empire, though Frederick was increasingly focused on Italian affairs and the conflict with the Papacy. His reign was characterized by attempts to strengthen imperial authority, though the power of the German princes was growing. Frederick's later years would see his involvement in the Third Crusade, which would ultimately lead to his death in 1190. In 1180, the empire was at a high point of territorial extent and administrative organization.
events.certainty_note: Frederick Barbarossa's reign and the general political situation in 1180 are well-documented in contemporary German chronicles.

events.title: Jin Dynasty Stability Under Emperor Shizong
events.description: In 1180, the Jin Dynasty (established by the Jurchen in 1115) maintained firm control over northern China under Emperor Shizong (reigned 1161–1189). The Jin Dynasty was at a relatively stable point, with a well-organized administrative system and military structure. The division of China between the Jin Dynasty in the north and the Song Dynasty in the south was a defining feature of 12th-century East Asian geopolitics. The Jin Dynasty represented a significant non-Han conquest state and facilitated cultural and technological exchange between the steppe and settled Chinese civilization. The Jin court at Zhongdu (modern Beijing) was a center of cultural and intellectual activity.
key_figures: Jin court officials and military commanders

sources.name: Song Dynasty records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The political situation in 1180 is well-documented in both Jin and Song records. The Jin Dynasty's control of northern China is securely established.

events.title: Khmer Empire Under Jayavarman VI (Pre-Jayavarman VII)
events.description: In 1180, the Khmer Empire was in the final years of the reign of Jayavarman VI (or possibly transitioning to Jayavarman VII, depending on the chronology). The empire was at a high point of territorial extent and cultural achievement. The transition to Jayavarman VII, who would ascend to the throne around 1181, would mark a shift from Hindu to Buddhist religious dominance and initiate a period of intensive architectural development. In 1180, the Khmer Empire controlled much of mainland Southeast Asia and maintained extensive trade networks with China and other regions.
events.certainty_note: The chronology of Khmer rulers in the late 12th century is uncertain. The transition from Jayavarman VI to Jayavarman VII is traditionally dated to c. 1181, but the exact year is uncertain by ±2–3 years.

disconfirming_evidence: The death of Taira no Kiyomori is traditionally dated to 1181, not 1180, in most Japanese sources. Some sources place it in 1180, but 1181 is more commonly cited. The exact year is uncertain by ±1 year. Additionally, the chronology of Khmer rulers in the late 12th century is uncertain, and the transition from Jayavarman VI to Jayavarman VII may have occurred in 1180 or 1181, with modern scholarship favoring c. 1181.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1180 is dominated by European, Islamic, and East Asian sources, reflecting the documentary traditions of literate societies. Japanese sources provide detailed accounts of the Genpei War, though the narrative tradition (Heike Monogatari) was compiled centuries later and may contain legendary elements. Islamic sources on Saladin are contemporary and relatively reliable. Chinese and Korean records are well-preserved. Khmer inscriptional evidence provides information about the empire, though the chronology of rulers is less precise than for other regions. The sources reflect elite concerns—military campaigns, dynastic politics, and religious projects—and likely underrepresent economic and social developments among non-elite populations. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Oceania have no written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: The Genpei War beginning in 1180 culminated in the establishment of the Kamakura shogunate in 1185
Taira no Kiyomori's death weakened the Taira clan and contributed to their eventual defeat in 1185
Saladin's consolidation of Ayyubid power in 1180 enabled his conquest of Jerusalem in 1187
